author.name: Kendrick Chan
author.headline: UX/UI Designer | Figma | Prototyping | Data-Informed B2B & B2C SaaS Product Design | AI Tools (Figma Make, Builder, & Framer) | Agentic AI | Vibe Coding

posts.content:
Just designed an interactive CTI radar chart and heat map for security teams to track and risk exposure in real time. 

Here's how I built it using vibe coding workflows:

- Figma Make: Turned static screens into interactive design-to-code prototypes.

- Cursor: Generated Markdown files via AI prompts for rapid iteration.

- Markdown: Enforced design system guidelines for Figma Make’s AI output.

ai.title: Interactive CTI radar chart and heat map for security teams using vibe coding workflows
ai.summary: Designer built an interactive CTI radar chart using vibe coding workflows. Process involved Figma Make for design-to-code prototypes, Cursor for AI-generated Markdown files, and Markdown for design system enforcement.
ai.why_important: Shows practical implementation of AI-assisted design-to-code workflows with design system consistency.
ai.learnings: How to use vibe coding workflows to create interactive prototypes while maintaining design system guidelines.

posts.content:
I had a conversation with our head of UX, Giovanni Piemontese which made me rethink the whole vibe design workflow.

The conventional flow goes like this: designer uses an AI agent in Figma to generate a mock, then engineers use that mock as the source of truth and translate it into code.

It works. But there's a handoff problem buried in it.

The Figma file is an intermediary artifact that has to be interpreted twice — once by the designer reviewing the AI output, and again by the engineer translating it into code. Each interpretation introduces drift. What the designer intended, what Figma captured, and what the engineer built can all diverge, even with AI assisting at every step.

What if we go the other way around and have designers started from an empty repo instead?

Designer starts from a blank repo, uses AI to vibe code the mock directly. They render it in the browser, give feedback to the AI, iterate. No Figma involved. Their deliverable to the engineer is the actual code — a simplified HTML and JS file that represents exactly what they designed, rendered exactly as they intended. In practice they can also provide past design as context to improve design consistency. 

The engineer then asks AI to take that mock and translate it into the existing system — matching the component library, the design tokens, the architecture. No MCP server is needed. The mock isn't a picture of what to build. It's a working prototype of it.

— Thanks to Giovanni Piemontese for the conversation that sparked this.

ai.title: Rethinking vibe design workflow: conversation with head of UX about handoff problems
ai.summary: Discussion about problems in conventional AI design workflow where Figma serves as intermediary artifact requiring dual interpretation. Identifies drift issues between designer review and engineer implementation.
ai.why_important: Highlights critical workflow inefficiencies in AI-assisted design handoffs.
ai.learnings: Understanding the interpretation drift problem in AI-generated design handoffs and potential solutions.

author.name: Ivan Zubko
author.headline: CG Technical Director | Unreal Engine | Motion Designer| Cinematic Artist | 3D Generalist

posts.content:
Unreal Engine vs. AI:

I tested two completely different workflows to create this locomotive transition effect:

🔹 Top (Traditional): Unreal Engine (Materials + Niagara particle systems). ⏱️ Time: Half a day. Result: Offers absolute control, but iterative adjustments are highly time-consuming.

🔹 Bottom (Generative AI): ⏱️ Time: 30 minutes. Result: A massive productivity boost. At 1/8th the time, it delivered a clean, detailed result.

My Takeaway: Honestly, I'm left in total confusion about what the "better" approach actually is now. AI just feels so much easier and faster, making me question when to even justify the traditional grind for tasks like this.

What do you guys think? Is the traditional way still worth the time, or is AI just the obvious choice now? Let's discuss in the comments! 👇
#UnrealEngine #GenerativeAI #VFX #TechnicalArt #MotionDesign #WorkflowEfficiency

ai.title: Unreal Engine vs. AI: locomotive transition effect comparison - Traditional vs Generative AI workflows
ai.summary: Technical director compared traditional Unreal Engine workflow (half day) vs AI workflow (30 minutes) for locomotive transition effect. Traditional offers control but slow iteration, AI delivers 8x speed improvement.
ai.why_important: Direct comparison shows dramatic productivity gains while questioning trade-offs between control and speed.
ai.learnings: Practical time comparison between traditional 3D workflows and AI alternatives for specific effects.

author.name: Julia Madsen
author.headline: High-Fidelity AI Video Production | AI Corporate Training & Courses

posts.content:
Let me be honest about what AI film is not.

It's not a magic button.
You don't type a prompt and get a finished commercial.

It still requires creative direction.
Story. Visual language. Brand understanding.
Sound design. Editing. A real eye for what works.

The AI handles the technical execution.
The human handles the thinking.

This is why not every AI video looks cinematic.
Most look generated — flat, generic, forgettable.

The difference is whether someone with a background in visual production is directing the process.

I spent years working in 3D visualisation and design before I ever touched an AI tool.
That knowledge is what makes the output look like film — not like a tech demo.

If you're evaluating AI video for your business:
ask to see the work, not just the technology. 
#aimovie #aifilm #AIreklamefilm #aivideo

ai.title: Let me be honest about what AI film is not - Creative direction still required for cinematic results
ai.summary: Experienced film professional explains that AI isn't a magic button - requires creative direction, story, visual language, and brand understanding. AI handles technical execution while humans handle creative thinking.
ai.why_important: Clarifies the role division between AI and human creativity in professional film production.
ai.learnings: Understanding what makes AI video look cinematic vs generic - importance of visual production background.

author.name: Asep Yuhana
author.headline: 3D Artist | Architectural Visualization | 3d visualizer | Virtual Reality / Augmented Reality Specialist | 20+ Years in Immersive & Engineering Visualization | Generative images and video Ai

posts.content:
🎬 AI Cinematic Action Short Film – Created with AI Tools

What happens when cinematic storytelling meets AI creativity?

I experimented with creating a short action film sequence set in a rain-soaked cargo port at night — combining visual storytelling, atmosphere, and high-intensity action.

⚡ Thunder in the background
🌧️ Heavy rain environment
🎥 Dynamic camera angles and cinematic lighting
💥 Gunfight and close combat sequences

This project was created using an AI-assisted production workflow:

🖼️ Image generation: Nanobanana
🎞️ Video generation: Veo Flow grok
✂️ Editing & compositing: CapCut and After Effects
🎧 Sound & music: Suno

My focus in this project was exploring how AI tools can support cinematic storytelling, from character consistency to shot composition, pacing, and action choreography.

AI is not replacing creativity — it’s expanding what creators can produce.

I’m excited to keep exploring how AI + filmmaking techniques can create compelling visual stories.

Would love to hear your thoughts! 🚀

#AI #AIVideo #CinematicAI #Filmmaking #VideoEditing #CreativeAI #GenerativeAI

posts.content:
AI didn’t make this… but it did make it move.  

I’ve been making analog collages for years, the texture, the fibers, the rips, the imperfections... that’s what gives them life. ✂️ 

Lately, I’ve been thinking a lot about how AI is changing creativity. With Firefly, I can take a scanned collage and bring it to life in motion, something I could imagined while creating, but would have taken hours to animate manually. AI doesn’t create the art for me, it helps me explore it in new dimensions, faster. 

In a time when the creative process is evolving, AI is amplifying our ideas and giving us the freedom to experiment in ways that weren’t possible before. 

The art was already there. AI just set it in motion.

Adobe #Firefly #MobileApp #AiDesign #DesignCommunity #DesignProcess

ai.title: AI didn't make this… but it did make it move - Analog collages brought to life with Firefly
ai.summary: Adobe Sr. Product Designer combines traditional analog collage creation with AI motion. Uses Firefly to animate scanned collages, preserving texture and imperfections while adding motion dimension.
ai.why_important: Shows how AI can enhance rather than replace traditional creative processes in motion design.
ai.learnings: Hybrid workflow combining analog art creation with AI-powered motion enhancement.

posts.content:
I’m excited to share that Turntable (one of my favorite new workflows) is now generally available in Adobe Illustrator! 
 
First previewed as a MAX Sneak, Turntable has been refined with input from the creative community to deliver  powerful new way for designers and animators to bring their artwork to life. 
 
With Turntable, you can instantly generate multi-angle views from any vector illustration, bringing motion to characters and designs, and eliminate the need for repetitive manual redraws. What once took hours now happens in seconds, making it easier and more fun for designers and animators to turn static artwork into dynamic, expressive visuals. 
 
Customers are already telling us how transformative this has been for their creative workflows.  Animation teams can quickly create character turnarounds for pitch decks, game designers can generate 360-degree assets for concept art and social media teams can create GIFs and micro-animations in seconds, all within Illustrator. And with seamless handoff to tools like After Effects, teams can move from design to motion without breaking their flow. 
 
Turntable is part of a broader set of Illustrator innovations, from faster performance to expanded AI-powered workflows like Generative Shape Fill and more seamless motion pipelines with After Effects. These innovations are all designed to help you move faster, explore more ideas, and stay in your creative flow. 
 
More details below. Can’t wait to see what you all create! 

https://lnkd.in/gKWSvFGz

ai.title: Turntable is now generally available in Adobe Illustrator - multi-angle views from vector illustrations
ai.summary: Adobe VP announces Turntable feature in Illustrator that generates multi-angle views from vector illustrations. Eliminates manual redrawing for character animation and brings motion to designs in seconds.
ai.why_important: Significant workflow improvement for designers and animators working with vector-based motion.
ai.learnings: How Turntable automates multi-angle view generation for vector animation workflows.

author.name: Keerthi Vardhan
author.headline: Web Developer | Helping Brands Build Fast, Modern Websites | Exploring AI & App Development

posts.content:
AI can generate layouts, components, and even full websites now. But when it comes to building high-quality animated web experiences, it still falls short.

I’m currently working on a project where I’m experimenting with GSAP, and it made something clear — the gap between generating code and crafting motion on the web is still huge.

AI can scaffold things quickly, but complex animation workflows, timing control, and scroll-driven interactions still require real problem solving.

The narrative that AI will instantly replace creative web development feels exaggerated.

Tools will keep improving, but developers who understand motion and interaction design are still very much in the game.

#GSAP #WebAnimation #FrontendDevelopment #AI #WebDevelopment

ai.title: AI can generate layouts but falls short on animated web experiences - GSAP animation workflow gaps
ai.summary: Web developer experimenting with GSAP identifies gap between AI code generation and crafting quality motion on web. Complex animation workflows, timing control, and scroll-driven interactions still require human problem solving.
ai.why_important: Realistic assessment of current AI limitations in web animation and motion design.
ai.learnings: Understanding where AI excels (scaffolding) vs where human expertise remains essential (complex motion).

author.name: Lucy L.
author.headline: Ai-1st marketing🌟 | Ex-okx/Alibaba/Ledger| East × West | Scaling Brands from $0 → $10M+✨|1M+ views on LinkedIn

posts.content:
I use AI motion design videos to double the view rate of my clients' ads. :)

Here's the uncomfortable truth about content in 2026:
Being on-brand is no longer enough. Being beautiful is no longer enough. AI has made "good looking" the baseline for everyone.

The only content that still wins is content that entertains, surprises, or teaches something people didn't know.

Motion design does all three. It used to cost a fortune. Now it doesn't.
I made this video to explain the gut-brain connection — probably the most important science of the last 20 years that nobody is talking about clearly.

Built with Pletor. In 2 hours. Alone.
The workflow: 
→ Claude for narrative and script 
→ Nano Banana for storyboard 
→ Veo 3.1 for animation 
→ Pletor nodes for final edit and subtitles
→ ElevenLabs for VO effect.
ALL WITHIN ONE PLATFORM.

If you have a complex product, a dry subject, or a message that never seems to land visually — this format is for you.
Comment 👇 "GUT POWER" and I'll share the exact template. You can ship your first video in the next 2 hours.

This art work is a collab with minimiil ✨
